1:
šîr hamaʿălôt — "a song of the ascents" — the final psalm bearing this superscription, closing the collection that opened at Ps 120. maʿălôt (ascents, steps, goings up) from ʿālāh (to go up, ascend). This last Ascent psalm is unique in its brevity and bidirectional structure: vv1–2 address the temple servants; v3 is their priestly response. bārĕkû YHWH kol-ʿabdê YHWH — "bless the LORD, all servants of the LORD" — bārak in the Piel (bless) is used of both humans blessing God (adoration) and God blessing humans (benefit); here human praise directed toward the divine. ʿabdê YHWH (servants of the LORD) — the term denotes temple personnel, specifically the Levitical night watch who maintained the sanctuary when the festival pilgrims had departed.

šeʿōmĕdîm bĕbêt-YHWH ballêlôt — "who stand in the house of the LORD through the nights" — ʿāmad (to stand) is the technical term for priestly service (Deuteronomy 10:8; Psalms 17:12). The plural ballêlôt (by night, plural of laylāh) emphasizes continuous nocturnal duty, not a single evening. The night-watch Levites kept vigil, guarded the precincts, and conducted overnight ritual duties (1 Chronicles 9:27; Nehemiah 11:19).
2: śĕʾû yĕdêkem qōdeš ûbārĕkû ʾet-YHWH — "lift your hands toward the holy [place] and bless the LORD" — śāʾû (Qal imperative of nāśāʾ, lift, carry) + yĕdêkem (your hands, dual). The gesture of uplifted hands (nāśāʾ yādayim) is the standard posture of blessing and supplication across the ancient Near East — both petition and praise involve the opened, raised hands (1 Kings 8:22; Nehemiah 8:6; 1 Timothy 2:8). qōdeš (holy, the Holy Place / sanctuary) — the word may be adverbial ("in holiness") or directional ("toward the sanctuary"), pointing the hands toward the inner sanctuary even during the night watch.
3: yĕbārekĕkā YHWH miṣṣiyyôn ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "may the LORD bless you from Zion — the maker of heaven and earth" — the priestly response to vv1–2. yĕbārekĕkā (jussive, may he bless you) — the blessing flows from the LORD enthroned in Zion. ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) — this precise formula closes Psalms 121 (v2) and 124 (v8), and now 134 (v3), creating a structural bracket across the entire Ascents collection. The pilgrim who started with "my help comes from the LORD, maker of heaven and earth" (Psalms 121:2) ends with the same divine title in a priestly benediction. The one worshipped in Zion is not a local deity but the creator of everything — the cosmic frame holds the pilgrimage together.
